commentBody:
I've learned in my brief tenure here that there are two ways to deal with a Kugelman grid:

1.  Skip it.  I'm too OCD/unevolved for this.
2.  Imagine an AI programmed to mimic a very clever spectrum-dwelling dad who lives to amuse only himself with puns and jokes.  Next, take a deep breath and grab a cup of strong coffee or glass of peated whisky.  (Coffee for me, usually, as I'm a morning solver and am frequently off to work soon after...)  Finally, commence to solve in the knowledge you will derive little pleasure apart from completing it.
I'm not touching this.

My favourite kind of end of week puzzle where after an hour of struggle nothing seems to be working and I'm contemplating retiring from solving crosswords and questioning all my life choices and drafting a mini rant in my head to rail at the random trivia and seemingly unsolvable Naticks and suddenly everything falls into place and the gold star pops up. Loved it.

Sort of got the theme in that these were repeating letters. Interesting. But the rest of it - just no. Way too much archane trivia. Looked up about half of it. There needs to be a limit on trivia. Isn't this why we have editors?

I didn't enjoy this puzzle. Too many obscure words and trivia. This was the worst puzzle I've seen in a while.

Loved the theme answers, but some of the non-theme clues nearly killed me! It took a full hour to solve this bad boy without lookups. The last two sections fall:

First, crossing GENA and PEKOE with AIKIDO. This was more or less a guess based on how a woman’s name, an Indonesian word, and a Japanese word might be spelled.  

Second, TWERE crossed with EWER and ARE — merciless, short answers to clues which I found impenetrable. In the end, the vaguest memory of Hamlet saying “to hold, as t’were” made me fill it in, confident that I would get the alert telling me I’d made a mistake. Instead the puzzle was solved, at which point I went to Google to look up what an “ewer” was, and came to this here comments section to understand the “Modern art?” pun (clever!)

Theme answers were very fun but the crosses were very much not. As others have noted, the PEKOE/AIKIDO area felt simply unfair. A bizarre amount of the trivia was music-related too. Overall this one was not a good experience.

This was fun at times. Ha Ha Yeah she said. But I cannot recall taking over an hour on a crossword in this century. I loved the theme answers, though they made me feel a little cross-eyed with all the repeating characters. But that was the whole point.

Found on the internet: Re 93D [Modern art?] and "O Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo" [Spoken by Juliet in Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet, this famous line means "Why are you Romeo?" Contrary to popular belief, she isn't asking for his location; she is lamenting that he is a Montague, as their feuding families make their love impossible.]

Who knew?

A side note on XENO, which we now associate with xenophobia:

I remember from studying Ancient Greek at school that xenos (ξένος) confusingly means “guest, host or stranger” — two of those words always appeared to me to be opposites. 

Wikipedia elaborates on the ambiguity, telling me that xenos can refer to friends whose relationship is described as xenia (guest-friendship), in contrast to a friend who was a philos (φίλος), a word used to refer to local friends and to relatives not strictly bound by xenia. 

The Greeks used this ambiguity because they thought strangers could be gods or goddesses in disguise, so they were always kind and respectful to strangers, because if it were a god, they could be blessed by that god or goddess.

Food for thought on this beautiful Sunday.

Sometimes I like silly, sometimes I don’t. Sometimes its out-of-the-box-ness charms me. Other times, it simply makes me cringe. The goofiness in the box today fully charmed me.

I like that the theme employed two types of wordplay. One was the silliness caused by manipulation – coming up with long complete sentences using only four letters. The other was the punning of “characters” in the revealer.

The first type made me go “Hah!” The second made me go, “Good one!”

So, I was entertained. But my brain was happy for another reason. It had hills – no-knows and tricky cluing – to conquer. A Kugelman Sunday often does this.

Thus, you doubled my pleasure, John. And please, just keep on doing what you do, as I love your bonkers voice. Thank you!

This was a fun puzzle! Clever theme. Were there names I didn’t know, yes (An actress from Baywatch?), but I was able to fill them in from the surrounding fill without looking anything up. I’m reading a lot of complaints about congee, I’m not Asian but I watch Top Chef and other culinary shows.  Isn’t the point of doing the crossword to exercise our brains and learn new things, not only fill in information we already know?

Thinking of Pink Floyd this morning:  Tongue-tied and twisted, just an earth-bound misfit ....

Second straight stellar Sunday.  Alliterative bliss.

Last night at 7pm, I was sitting on my stoop enjoying the drop in humidity and struggling with this puzzle. A woman with her teenaged daughter and dog walked by. I must have been sighing, and as she glanced over at me, she stopped and said “oh, this one is tough! I nearly gave up, but I got there.” And I thought I need to know this person who finishes Sunday’s puzzle by 7pm on Saturday night!

Took me almost 90 minutes, and it defeated me. I had entered WOWIE instead of YOWIE, and failed to check the crossing entry before filling the last box. (Though I should point out that quite often I say HA HA, WE AH when I nervously respond to "Are we in agreement, pardnah?" Must be a Pennsylvania thing ...)

Upon reviewing the grid, the fill is actually pretty decent given the demands of the theme. Except, perhaps, for MADEATRY. That said, this theme was feeling forced to me even before the truly terrible DERRIERE RIDER DERIDED made its appearance.

Well, the good news is that the rest of the day can only get better from here. Hope others (someone) enjoyed this puzzle.
While I appreciate how much skill this must have taken to construct, solving it felt like pulling teeth for me and I couldn’t wait for it to be over.

That was brutal. I think the hardest Sunday I’ve grappled with in some time. I really wanted to love it, but honestly, for me the theme was a tad meh, as my offspring would say, but then I’m not a pun lover, so that’s on me.

What I did like; interesting fill with little glue. A few gimmes like CONGEE, YVES and AGATES helped.

‘‘Twas International Gin Day yesterday. The gin club celebrated with a free party for allcomers; food and gin flowed, music was played by our resident folk duo. Even the sun shone. Marvellous. I’m sure my brain fog today has nothing to do with the activities of yesterday.

The rest of the fill was underwhelming. HA HA, YEAH?  GEEK GIRL CON? MADE A TRY? Then there's the sad UNALIVED. But a kudo, just one, for LEHRER.

Kahn made it into print this past week, but then Mr. Kugelman seems to have ousted all the rest of the brilliant Sunday constructors, and for the life of me, I don't know why.

First full morning of vacation Up North, and the [pie] is cooling on the SILL (rhubarb, with a couple of Granny Smith apples thrown in); No [Scottish cryptids] seen on Little Wolf Lake, although I spotted the resident loons, with a loonlet so small as to resemble a floating champagne cork. When would one need to measure electrical resistance in millions of ohms? (honest question). Of course, when would one need to measure personal wealth in teradollars? Looking up "megaohm" on DuckDuckGo, I see that it is also called a "megohm," eliding the "a" in "mega-," which the Hellenist in me likes. Also, www.swiftulator.com offers a service to "easily convert megaohm to ohm." "Are we really so stupid? O tempor', o mores!" he asked, eliding the "a." Here's a cover of "Take on me," performed by a small cast of characters--four to be precise--one of whom reads the C-CLEF. (Two actually, if you put it on the fourth line of the staff.) The VERY CREATIVE video, however, features a very *large* cast of characters, or at least tiles: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C_tFPN0KjQw&list=RDC_tFPN0KjQw&start_radio=1

Anytime I see the name John Kugelman on a puzzle, I know I'm in for both a treat and a workout. Today was no exception. Loved the theme and thought the cluing was challenging but oh so clever and fun.

Genuinely surprised by the strong negative reactions.  Seemed pretty typical for a Sunday puzzle.

Definitely didn't like the misspelling of megohm, regardless of the dictionary entry. Miriam-Webster agrees with me on this one.

Retired engineer here, and never in my life have I heard the full prefix used for the units of resistance.

Units in the range of megohms are pretty common, though. Anyone designing with opamps would use values in that range pretty regularly.

For what it’s worth, “obscure” is always in the eye of the beholder, and can be very different depending on your demographic. UNALIVED is extremely common internet speak, sometimes to earnestly avoid demonetization but more often as a tongue-in-cheek reference. I had CONGEE multiple times in the last few months here in the Bay Area, and generally feel like it’s on the rise as a brunch phenomenon. (To say nothing of Chinese immigrant families who have been eating it since long before it was “on trend”, of course.)
Will, what were you thinking? 59 across sums up how I feel: See ya!

I thought  99A was XENU, though AIKIDU didn't really feel right.

What a wonderful puzzle. Some of my favorite clues:

"Demonetization" which looks so much like "demonization" (but isn't)

The monetary symbol that can stand for two different currencies (One of which took forever to remember)

"Measure of high resistance" (which I originally filled with a misspelled version of the measuring instrument)

Stargazer's channel (I miss you!)

The clue for IMPS (what wonderful mental imagery)

Learning a fact about Sam SNEAD (again, wonderful imagery)

The washstand item (hazy mental imagery of a shaving cup)

And finally, the constellation of answers that really elevated this puzzle for me: the fandom event and washstand item crossing the Hamlet quotation. Because I got the fandom event late in my solve, I had the very tantalizing TWE_E sitting there for the longest time. Getting that missing R was the satisfying end to a perfect meal.

Sometimes I like silly, sometimes I don’t.  Sometimes its out-of-the-box-ness charms me.  Other times, it simply makes me cringe.  The goofiness in the box today fully charmed me.

I like that the theme employed two types of wordplay.  One was the silliness caused by manipulation – coming up with long complete sentences using only four letters.  The other was the punning of “characters” in the revealer.

The first type made me go “Hah!”  The second made me go, “Good one!”

So, I was entertained.  But my brain was happy for another reason.  It had hills – no-knows and tricky cluing – to conquer.  A Kugelman Sunday often does this.

Thus, you doubled my pleasure, John.  And please, just keep on doing what you do, as I love your bonkers voice.  Thank you!

The more time I spend digesting this puzzle, the more I am warming toward it and its constructor.

One of our core group of friends in college was a huge dweeb.  

He moped about constantly, complained non stop, never wanted to do anything exciting, told awful jokes and made dreadful puns, had super-annoying bathroom habits better left unmentioned.  He was brash, abrupt and unkempt. When he'd get drunk we'd tie his shoelaces together and watch him stand, fall, get up, repeat and we'd die laughing (we were drunk, too, of course).  

On the other hand he was great at trivia (we were College Bowl contenders), had a razor-sharp analytical mind and he always contributed just what you'd need him to on any given occasion.  And he always showed up when he said he would.

We always missed him when he wasn't around even though he always drove us nuts when he was.  (Imagine the Daniel Stern character in the classic film Diner.)

Kugelman grids might just be beginning to feel like the puzzle-equivalent of my old college pal.
